content: Adverse drug reactions (ADRs) are noxious, unintended responses to drugs occurring at doses normally used for prophylaxis, diagnosis, or therapy. Type A reactions (augmented) are predictable, dose-dependent, and related to pharmacological effects (e.g., bleeding with anticoagulants, hypoglycemia with insulin, sedation with benzodiazepines). They account for 80-90% of ADRs. Type B reactions (bizarre) are unpredictable, not dose-dependent, often immune-mediated (e.g., anaphylaxis, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, drug-induced lupus). Type C (chronic), D (delayed), E (end-of-use/withdrawal), and F (failure of efficacy) are additional categories. The Rawlins-Thompson classification guides prevention and management strategies.
